nuggets.headline: Radiohead's sonic fingerprint on 'In Rainbows' comes from a deliberate rejection of the modern studio, favoring derelict spaces and a rare Motown-era console.
nuggets.text: Producer Nigel Godrich, often called the band's sixth member, steered the sessions away from traditional studios and into Tottenham House, a dilapidated mansion in Wiltshire. This unconventional space provided cavernous natural reverb that permeates the album. Godrich paired this organic ambiance with a 24-channel Audiotronix console he acquired from a Los Angeles studio, which was allegedly used on a number of Motown records, directly contributing to the album's signature warmth and saturated guitar tones.

source.title: Engineering the Sound: Radiohead's 'In Rainbows'
source.quoteSnippet: Godrich took the boys on a little excursion to a decrepit estate called Tottenham House... The cavernous mansion of 100-plus rooms offered an endless source of natural reverb for the album.

nuggets.headline: The propulsive momentum of 'Weird Fishes/Arpeggi' is a masterful illusion built on a foundation that never changes.
nuggets.text: Listen closely to Phil Selway's drumming; the core beat remains almost entirely static throughout the song's five-minute duration. The powerful feeling of crescendo is not achieved through rhythmic shifts, but through the meticulous layering of multiple, interlocking arpeggiated guitar lines from Thom Yorke, Jonny Greenwood, and Ed O'Brien. This technique of building textural density over a constant rhythmic pulse is heavily influenced by minimalist composers like Steve Reich, creating a complex, shifting web of sound that pulls the listener forward.

source.title: A Harmonic Analysis of Radiohead's “Weird Fishes/Arpeggi”
source.quoteSnippet: The track starts with a solid drum groove, which will stay the same throughout most of the song. This choice is quite interesting since it implies that the drums will not signal the subdivision of the arrangement in sections... Alternatively, the way Radiohead create momentum is done in a textural way: the density of the arrangement increases with the addition of several layers.

nuggets.headline: If the intimate, lush production of this album resonates with you, you owe it to yourself to hear where producer Nigel Godrich perfected that sound five years earlier.
nuggets.text: Before 'In Rainbows', Godrich produced Beck's 2002 masterpiece, 'Sea Change'. It's a clear sonic precursor, showcasing Godrich's developing mastery of capturing raw, emotional performances with immense atmospheric depth. You can hear the DNA of 'In Rainbows' in 'Sea Change's rich acoustic textures, spacious reverbs, and the focus on a live band's organic chemistry, making it an essential listen for understanding the production lineage of this sound.

source.title: Nigel Godrich - Wikipedia
source.quoteSnippet: Godrich produced three albums by the American singer-songwriter Beck: Mutations (1998), Sea Change (2002) and The Information (2006). The first two, particularly Sea Change, were noted for their atmospheric folk/pop sound, a departure from the spontaneous, sample-heavy style Beck was known for.

nuggets.imageCaption: Radiohead performing live — producer Nigel Godrich shaped their 'In Rainbows' sound
